title: Quick check: Volumes vs bind mounts

questions.prompt: When should you use a named volume vs a bind mount?

choices.text: Volumes for production data; bind mounts for development. Volumes are Docker-managed (lives in /var/lib/docker/volumes), portable, survive container lifecycle, no host-path coupling. Bind mounts tie containers to specific host paths and inherit host UID/permission semantics — useful for mounting source code in dev for hot reload, but fragile for production. Always volume-mount database data directories so data survives container recreation. tmpfs for sensitive temp data that shouldn't touch disk.

questions.explanation: Volumes for production data, bind mounts for dev, tmpfs for sensitive temp.

questions.prompt: A team runs Postgres in Docker. Data disappears on container restart. Why?

choices.text: No volume mounted at /var/lib/postgresql/data. Without a volume, the database files live in the container's writable layer, which is destroyed when the container is removed. Each `docker run postgres` is a fresh database. Fix: mount a named volume at the data directory (`-v pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data`). The volume persists across container removals; data survives. Common mistake; obvious in hindsight; bites everyone the first time.

questions.explanation: Stateful workloads need volume-mounted data directories. Containers are ephemeral; volumes persist.
